verifier_values.criteria: States that University of Texas Southwestern Med Center v. Nassar held that “but for” causation is used to prove Title VII retaliation claims
verifier_values.criteria_explanation: University of Texas Southwestern Med Center v. Nassar, 570 U.S. 338 (2013) held that “but for” causation is used to prove Title VII retaliation claims. “This requires proof that the unlawful retaliation would not have occurred in the absence of the alleged wrongful action or actions of the employer.”

verifier_values.criteria: States that, according to Bostock v. Clayton County, the but-for causation test does not require that the prohibited factor be the sole or primary reason for the adverse action
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
In Bostock, the Court stated, "the adoption of the traditional but-for causation standard means a defendant cannot avoid liability just by citing some other factor that contributed to
its challenged employment decision. So long as the plaintiff ’s sex was one but-for cause of that decision, that is enough to trigger the law." The CFR commentary states, "Adding ‘‘or primary’’
further emphasizes the Supreme Court’s holdings and reflects the language in Bostock that the protected factor need not be the primary reason for the adverse action. See Bostock, 140 S Ct. at
1739."

verifier_values.criteria: State that 29 CRF 1977.6(b) was updated to reflect the Court's decisions in Nassar and Bostock
verifier_values.criteria_explanation: Effective September 3, 2021, OSHA amended 29 CRF 1977.6(b) to bring it in line with the Supreme Court’s holdings in Nassar and Bostock. The commentary related to the updates states that the plaintiff must show that but for the protected activity, the employee would not have suffered the adverse action. Second, the rule contains citations to Nassar and Bostock. Third, the rule was amended to emphasize the Court’s holding in Bostock that the protected factor need not be the primary reason for the adverse action. "This final interpretive rule updates OSHA’s 1973 section 11© interpretive rule at 29 CFR 1977.6(b) to bring it in line with the Supreme Court’s holdings in Gross, Nassar, and Bostock."

verifier_values.criteria: States that Porter must prove “but-for” causation
verifier_values.criteria_explanation: After the Court’s rulings in Bostock and Nassar, as well as OSHA’s changes to 29 CRF 1977.6(b), it is clear that the Porter must prove “but-for” causation. University of Texas Southwestern Med Center v. Nassar, 570 U.S. 338 (2013) held that “but for” causation is used to prove Title VII retaliation claims. “This requires proof that the unlawful retaliation would not have occurred in the absence of the alleged wrongful action or actions of the employer.” The Court’s reasoning was based upon the word choice “because” in the statue, which is also used in 11(c)(1). In Bostock v. Clayton County, 590 U. S. 644, (2020), The Court explained that but-for causation is established whenever a particular outcome would not have happened ‘‘but for’’ the purported cause. The Court stated that but-for causation test ‘‘directs us to change one thing at a time and see if the outcome changes. If it does, we have found a but-for cause.’’ Effective September 3, 2021, OSHA amended 29 CRF 1977.6(b) to bring it in line with the Supreme Court’s holdings in Nassar and Bostock.

verifier_values.criteria: States that Porter does not need to prove that the protected factor was the primary reason for his being fired to prevail
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
As stated in section 1977.6(b), an employer’s mixed motivation for an adverse action does not necessarily invalidate an employee’s section 11(c) complaint. See 29 CFR
1977.6(b) (‘‘[T]o establish a violation of section 11(c), [a]n employee’s engagement in protected activity need not be the sole consideration behind discharge or other adverse action.’’).
